title: Burns
summary: Burns can be caused by heat, chemicals, electricity, or radiation. Quick action is crucial to minimize damage.
steps: Remove the person from the source of heat or burn immediately.
Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water for at least 10–20 minutes.
Remove any jewelry, watches, or clothing near the burn area — but NOT if stuck to skin.
Cover the burn loosely with a sterile, non-fluffy bandage or clean cloth.
Do NOT apply butter, toothpaste, oil, or ice to the burn.
Give over-the-counter pain relief (e.g. paracetamol) if available.
Keep the person warm to prevent shock while cooling the burn.
what_to_do: Cool the burn with running water for 10–20 minutes
Use a clean, non-fluffy covering to protect the area
Keep the person warm and calm
Seek medical help for large, deep, or facial burns
what_not_to_do: Do NOT use ice, iced water, or cold packs
Do NOT apply butter, oil, toothpaste, or creams
Do NOT burst any blisters that form
Do NOT remove clothing stuck to skin
Do NOT leave the person unattended if severely burned
when_to_call_ambulance: Burns larger than the size of the person's palm
Burns on the face, hands, feet, genitals, joints, or a large area of the body
The person is having difficulty breathing
Child or elderly person is burned
